skill.name: android-native-dev
skill.description: Android native application development guide for Kotlin, Jetpack Compose, Material Design 3, Gradle setup, accessibility, testing, and release quality.
skill.summary: Build Android native apps with Kotlin, Compose, Material Design 3, testing, accessibility, and Gradle guidance.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, crypto, and storage patterns, but review found they are Android documentation examples in Markdown. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or executable skill code was found. The skill is publishable with low risk because it contains build and device command examples users may ask an agent to run.

low_findings.title: Documentation Contains Android Build Command Examples
low_findings.description: Static command detections are Gradle, adb, or inline Android API examples inside Markdown guidance. They can affect a user project if executed, but the skill itself does not execute commands or hide command execution logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown instructions and fenced command examples. No code path in the skill runs these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Network Patterns Are Example Endpoints And Mocked Calls
low_findings.description: Network detections are sample BuildConfig URLs, coroutine examples, and mocked test calls. No evidence found of real external endpoints receiving user data or credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context shows placeholder example domains and test doubles. There is no semantic evidence of data exfiltration or active network behavior by the skill.

low_findings.title: Security Keywords Are Defensive Android Guidance
low_findings.description: Weak crypto and storage detections correspond to guidance that recommends encrypted storage, platform cryptography, and avoiding MD5 or SHA1 for security. These are protective recommendations rather than risky implementation code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text explicitly warns against weak algorithms and sensitive logging. The apparent storage and crypto hits are educational content, not executable secret handling.

content.value_statement: Native Android projects often fail because setup, UI, accessibility, testing, and release quality are handled separately. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code one practical guide for building and reviewing Kotlin Android apps.
actual_capabilities: Guides Android project setup with Gradle, AndroidX, build variants, and product flavors.
Explains Kotlin and coroutine patterns for ViewModels, repositories, state, and error handling.
Reviews Jetpack Compose usage, Material Design 3 structure, typography, spacing, and color contrast.
Provides accessibility, adaptive screen, privacy, performance, and Google Play quality checklists.
Outlines Android test layers, dependencies, Espresso, Compose UI tests, and managed device workflows.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not a complete Android application generator by itself.
Version examples may need updates for each project dependency catalog.
Build commands still require a local Android SDK, Gradle setup, and available devices or emulators.
It cannot verify Google Play policy compliance without reviewing the final app behavior and disclosures.

use_cases.title: Start a Compose App
use_cases.description: Create a Kotlin Android project structure with Gradle settings, Compose dependencies, resources, and a build-first workflow.

use_cases.title: Review Mobile UI Quality
use_cases.description: Check screens against Material Design 3, accessibility, adaptive layout, touch target, typography, and visual style guidance.

use_cases.title: Prepare Release Readiness
use_cases.description: Audit testing, performance, privacy, permissions, app icons, and Android Vitals before a release candidate.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the android-native-dev skill to create a minimal Kotlin Android project plan. Include Gradle files, AndroidX settings, package structure, and the first build command.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Compose Screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the android-native-dev skill to review this Compose screen for state handling, Material Design 3 structure, spacing, color contrast, and accessibility labels.

prompt_templates.title: Add Testing Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the android-native-dev skill to design a testing strategy for this Android app. Cover unit tests, Compose UI tests, Espresso tests, and managed devices.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Release Quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the android-native-dev skill to perform a release readiness review. Check privacy, permissions, performance, adaptive screens, accessibility, and Google Play quality risks.

output_examples.input: Review my new Android project setup before I add features.
output: A prioritized project setup checklist covering Gradle, AndroidX, package structure, resources, and build variants.
A list of missing files or risky defaults that should be fixed before feature work starts.
A recommended first verification step using the project build workflow.

output_examples.input: Check whether this Compose profile screen follows Android quality guidance.
output: A screen review organized by state management, layout hierarchy, Material Design 3 usage, accessibility, and adaptive behavior.
Specific UI issues such as small touch targets, unclear labels, contrast problems, or nonresponsive layouts.
Concrete next steps for improving the screen without changing the product intent.

output_examples.input: Plan tests for a Kotlin app with API data and offline caching.
output: A layered test plan that separates ViewModel, repository, database, UI, and instrumentation coverage.
Dependency alignment notes for coroutine, Compose, Espresso, and AndroidX test libraries.
A release confidence checklist tied to performance, crash risk, and user-visible workflows.

best_practices: Confirm the Android project builds before adding business logic or UI depth.
Match dependency versions to the project catalog and existing Gradle conventions.
Review accessibility, adaptive layouts, privacy, and tests as part of every feature.
anti_patterns: Adding test or UI dependencies without checking existing version alignment.
Treating phone layouts as final without tablet, foldable, and multi-window review.
Shipping debug logging, broad permissions, weak storage choices, or missing accessibility labels.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a full Android app?
faq.answer: It provides structured guidance and examples. The agent still needs your requirements and project context to implement the app.

faq.question: Can it help with Jetpack Compose?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Compose state, composable rules, Material Design 3 patterns, testing, and common implementation mistakes.

faq.question: Does it support XML-based Android views?
faq.answer: It focuses on modern native Android and Compose, but it includes general resource, accessibility, testing, and release guidance.

faq.question: Can it run Gradle or adb commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents useful commands. Your AI tool or local environment controls whether any command is executed.

faq.question: Is this useful for release preparation?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes privacy, permissions, accessibility, performance, Android Vitals, testing, and Google Play quality checks.

faq.question: What should I provide when using it?
faq.answer: Provide the project structure, Gradle files, target Android versions, UI goals, and any build or test errors.
